title: Spectral properties of non-Hermitian real random matrices with long-range correlations
abstract: We investigate the spectral properties of non-Hermitian real random matrices whose entries exhibit long-range correlations decaying as~$|r-r'|^{-α}$. We find a progressive breakdown of the circular law, controlled by the decrease of~$α$. In all cases, the radial eigenvalue density decreases away from the origin. At~$α>1$, an effective radius, reminiscent of the circular law, is retrieved, while instead, for~$α<1$, the eigenvalue distribution broadens with matrix size and its spectral radius grows like a power law, with exponents numerically close to the exponents controlling the magnitude of fluctuations in the extended central limit theorem. The case~$α=1$ appears as a case with self-similar eigenvalue density, and slowly growing spectral radius. Long-range correlations also enhance clustering of real eigenvalues and slow the resorption of the Saturn effect. These results reveal a correlation-driven transition and suggest the emergence of a new universality class for correlated non-Hermitian random matrices.
summary: This paper explores how long-range correlations in random matrices cause the standard circular law to break down, leading to a new universality class. It demonstrates that for strong correlations, the spectral radius grows according to a power law related to the extended central limit theorem. These findings have implications for the stability of large dynamical systems like ecosystems and neural networks.
main_problem: The study investigates how long-range correlations in the entries of non-Hermitian real random matrices affect their spectral properties and the breakdown of the circular law.
main_result: The authors identify a correlation-driven transition where the spectral radius grows as a power law for correlation exponents alpha < 1, and find that long-range correlations enhance the clustering of real eigenvalues.
method: Numerical simulations using a convolution-based sampling technique to generate large correlated matrices, followed by eigenvalue computation and statistical analysis of scaling laws.
model_or_system: Non-Hermitian real random matrices where entries exhibit Gaussian correlations decaying as a power law |r - r'|^(-alpha) on a 2D lattice.
key_observables: Spectral radius, radial eigenvalue density, eigenvalue spacing ratios (real and complex sectors), and the number of real eigenvalues.
important_parameters: Correlation exponent alpha (0 < alpha < 2) and matrix linear size L.
main_assumptions: The entries are assumed to be Gaussian with a specific power-law decay; the sampling method imposes periodic boundary conditions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares Ginibre vs. correlated spectra; Figure 2 shows 2D eigenvalue histograms and radial density distributions; Figure 3 displays spectral radius distributions; Figure 4 shows spectral radius scaling with L; Figure 5 presents eigenvalue spacing ratio distributions; Figure 6 shows the scaling of the number of real eigenvalues.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of correlated non-Hermitian matrices, describes the sampling methodology, presents the numerical results regarding the breakdown of the circular law and spectral radius scaling, analyzes eigenvalue spacing and real eigenvalue statistics, and concludes with implications for system stability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ginibre against correlated matrix. A) A 512 × 512 Ginibre matrix : no large-scale structure emerges. B) Matrix of the same dimension with long-range correlations, controlled by α = 0.1. Values in both matrices are displayed on the same color scales. C) Spectrum of the Ginibre matrix : each eigenvalue corresponds to a point, the dotted circle corresponds to the asymptotic limiting boundary given by the circular law. Note that the density seems almost uniform, except around the real axis ℑλ = 0, where eigenvalues are attracted by the line and deplete its surrounding : the ‘Saturn effect’. D) Spectrum of the matrix shown in B), the density is not uniform, but steeply decreasing with...

FIG. 2. Two-dimensional histogram of eigenvalues of 512 × 512 matrices for α = 0.1 (A), α = 1 (B) and α = 1.9 (C). The histograms were computed by computing the spectra of 5 · 104 matrices. Note that the spectra exist far beyond the limit imposed by the circle law, in all cases, as well as the scale of the real eigenvalues when α ≤1. D)E)F) Empirical distribution of the modulus of the eigenvalues P(|λ|) divided by 2π|λ|, for each value of α and for L varying between L = 32 and L = 512. The baseline is the limit circle law P(|λ|)/2π|λ| ∼1λ<1. At α < 1, the tail of the distribution broadens, while it narrows for α > 1. The case α = 1 appears as a self-similar case, as the densities collapse...

FIG. 3. Distribution of the spectral radius λm when varying, for α = 0.1 (A), α = 1 (B) and α = 1.9 (C). The position and scale of the distribution vary with L for α ≤1, while the distribution shrinks around a limit value (re(α = 1.9) ≈1.4), corresponding to an effective radius for a distorted circle law, at rate L−1/2 when α > 1. D)E)F) Skewness γ1 against matrix size L when varying α. Horizontal lines correspond to the skewness of baseline distributions: Gumbel (γ1 ≈1.14), Tracy-Widom for β = 2 (γ1 ≈0.22) and β = 1 (γ1 ≈0.29). The values of λm were computed from 5 · 104 realizations.
Fig. 1 shows the location of eigenvalues for one re- alization of the long-range correlated matrix ensemble,

FIG. 4. Scaling law for the spectral radius. Main panel: measured average spectral radius ⟨λm⟩in function of system size for two values of α given in the legend. The error bars represent the 95% confidence interval on the average. The dashed line represent the fit given by Eq. 9. The averages are computed over 5 · 104 realisations. Inset: measured scaling ˜γ (orange points) and finite-size ˜ω (green points) exponents in function of the correlation exponent α. Blue line: extended CLT exponent γ = 1−α
